Description: Histories, cultures, politics, and intellectual traditions of African-descended peoples in the U.S. and global diaspora — history, literature, sociology, and cultural studies.
Human societies, cultures, and biological evolution across time and place through cultural, biological, archaeological, and linguistic anthropology.
History, theory, and cultural significance of architecture and urban design, with emphasis on how the built environment shapes human experience and social life.
Studio training in drawing, painting, printmaking, sculpture, photography, and other media alongside study of art history and visual culture.

Majors: Asian American and Asian Diaspora Studies (B.A.)
Description: Histories, cultures, and social experiences of Asian-origin populations in the U.S. and globally through literary, historical, and sociological perspectives.
Interdisciplinary major covering civilizations, languages, histories, religions, and contemporary politics of East, South, and Southeast Asia.

Majors: Biochemistry and Chemical Biology (B.A. or B.S.)
Description: Chemical processes underlying living systems — molecular genetics, protein structure and function, enzyme mechanisms, and chemical basis of biological regulation. B.S. option added 2025–26.
Broad foundation in life sciences — cell biology, genetics, evolution, ecology, and physiology, with lab and field research opportunities.
Organic, inorganic, physical, and analytical chemistry for research, graduate study, medicine, or industry. B.S. option added 2025–26.
History and theory of film and media combined with hands-on practice in screenwriting, production, and editing.
Languages (Greek and Latin), literatures, histories, and material cultures of the ancient Greek and Roman worlds and the broader Mediterranean basin.
Interdisciplinary major on the scientific, social, political, and ethical dimensions of climate change and environmental sustainability.
How scientific and technical information is produced, communicated, and understood across public, professional, and media contexts.
Human communication in interpersonal, organizational, public, and mediated contexts — rhetoric, media, and social dimensions of communication practices.
B.A. pathway in computer science through the College of Connected Computing partnership; the ABET-accredited B.S. is offered through Engineering.
Interdisciplinary major focused on social change, community organizing, and civic engagement — sociology, political science, and cultural studies.

Majors: Earth and Environmental Sciences (B.A. or B.S.)
Description: Geological and geochemical processes shaping Earth — tectonics, geomorphology, hydrology, climate systems, and environmental change. B.S. option added 2025–26.
Allocation of scarce resources, market behavior, and macroeconomic systems — economic theory, quantitative methods, and applied policy analysis. B.S. option added 2025–26.
Combined major examining economic development and institutions through both economic modeling and historical analysis.
Literature in English from medieval to contemporary periods, with attention to literary form, cultural context, critical theory, and creative writing.
Interdisciplinary major on European politics, history, culture, languages, and societies from ancient times to the present.
Advanced proficiency in French alongside Francophone literature, culture, history, and linguistics across France and the global Francophone world.
Social construction of gender and sexuality, feminist theory, queer studies, and intersections with race, class, and power across cultures and history.
Advanced German with German, Austrian, and Swiss literature, culture, philosophy, and history from medieval to present.
The human past across diverse regions, periods, and themes using primary sources and historical methods, with strong research and writing training.
Visual arts — painting, sculpture, architecture, photography, and design — across world cultures and periods, combining formal analysis with historical interpretation.
Bridges classical biology with quantitative and computational methods, emphasizing organisms across scales from molecules to ecosystems. Major added 2025–26.
Interdisciplinary study of history, religion, literature, philosophy, and culture of Jewish communities from antiquity through the present.

Majors: Latin American, Caribbean, and Latinx Studies (B.A.)
Description: Histories, cultures, politics, and social movements of Latin America, the Caribbean, and Latinx communities in the U.S. from pre-Columbian times to the present.
Interdisciplinary major combining legal theory, historical analysis, and social science perspectives on how law shapes and is shaped by society.
Pure and applied mathematics — analysis, algebra, topology, probability, and statistics. Pathways into research, data science, and quantitative fields. B.S. option added 2025–26.
Social, cultural, historical, and political dimensions of health, medicine, and the body — preparation for health policy, medicine, and health-related social research.

Majors: Molecular and Cellular Biology (B.A. or B.S.)
Description: Molecular mechanisms underlying cellular function — gene expression, signal transduction, cell division, and molecular genetics. B.S. option added 2025–26.
Interdisciplinary integration of biology, chemistry, psychology, and computation to study the nervous system at molecular, cellular, systems, and behavioral levels. B.S. option added 2025–26.
Fundamental questions about knowledge, reality, ethics, logic, and mind — rigorous argumentation and conceptual analysis across major philosophical traditions.
Fundamental laws of matter and energy — classical mechanics, electromagnetism, quantum mechanics, and relativity, with lab and computational components. B.S. option added 2025–26.
Political institutions, behavior, theory, and international relations — American politics, comparative government, political philosophy, and global affairs.
Science of mind and behavior — cognitive, developmental, social, clinical science, and neuroscientific approaches to human psychology.
Interdisciplinary major on formulation, analysis, and implementation of public policy — economics, political science, law, and social science methods.
Diverse beliefs, practices, texts, rituals, and institutions of world religions through historical, literary, anthropological, and comparative approaches.
Russian language with Russian and Soviet literature, history, politics, culture, and the broader post-Soviet world.
Social structures, institutions, inequality, culture, and collective behavior — empirical methods for analyzing how societies are organized and change.
Advanced Spanish-language proficiency with literatures, cultures, histories, and linguistic diversity of Spain and Latin America.
Combined major developing proficiency in both Spanish and Portuguese with the interconnected literary and cultural traditions of the Iberian world and Latin America.
History and theory of theatre with studio practice in acting, directing, design, and dramaturgy — performance as an art form and cultural practice.

Engineering applied to biology and medicine — biomechanics, biomedical imaging, neural engineering, biomedical devices, and molecular/cellular bioengineering. Strong ties to Vanderbilt University Medical Center.
Transport phenomena, thermodynamics, reaction kinetics, separations, and process design with bench-scale and pilot-plant laboratory courses.
Structural analysis and design, geotechnical engineering, transportation, water resources, and construction management.
Integrates electrical engineering (circuits, signals, electromagnetics, photonics) and computer engineering (digital systems, embedded systems, microelectronics).
Mechanics, thermodynamics, fluid dynamics, materials, and design — applications across aerospace, robotics, manufacturing, energy, and biomedical systems.

ABET-accredited B.S. combining scientific and engineering principles with theoretical CS and practical programming — algorithms, systems, software engineering, AI, and data science. Also available as a B.A. through A&S.
Interdisciplinary program in the Division of Engineering Science and Management providing strong engineering and science foundations while allowing specialization in interdisciplinary interests.

College: Peabody College of Education and Human Development
Description: Vanderbilt's most popular undergraduate major. Interdisciplinary HOD/LPO program preparing students to address complex social problems in organizations and communities — psychology, organizational studies, sociology, political science, anthropology, and economics with required organizational capstone.
Scientific study of children from infancy through adolescence — biological, cognitive, social, and emotional development within family, peer, school, and cultural contexts.
Interdisciplinary major drawing on psychology, education, special education, and HOD for working with children in clinical, educational, and community settings outside formal schooling.
How people think, solve problems, learn, and reason — cognitive psychology, cognitive neuroscience, linguistics, and philosophy of mind.

Interdisciplinary scientific study of language structure, language use, and acquisition — linguistics, cognitive science, and psychology with relevance to NLP, multilingualism, healthcare, and education.
Research-grounded, practice-intensive teacher preparation for K–5 licensure with early classroom placements and a focus on children's developmental thinking and reasoning.
Teacher licensure preparation for grades 6–12 with concentrations in English, Mathematics, Natural Sciences, or Social Studies — subject-area methods plus supervised teaching practice.

Interdisciplinary, field-oriented teacher-licensure program for working with persons with disabilities. Specialty areas: High Incidence Disabilities (K–8 or 6–12); Multiple and Severe Disabilities. Ranked #1 nationally at the graduate level.

Classical performance at the highest level — orchestral instruments, piano, saxophone, euphonium, and voice. Individual instruction, solo recitals, master classes, chamber music, and ensembles with theory, musicianship, and music history.
Creation and analysis of music with deep exploration of repertoire and craft in art music, plus opportunities in film scoring, jazz composition, electroacoustic, computer, and experimental music.
Performance, composition, improvisation, musicianship, analysis, music technology, and entrepreneurship — instrumental or voice areas plus guitar and drum set, with specialized ensembles.

For strong performers and/or composers interested in other facets of music — primary performance/composition area plus a secondary concentration (musicology, ethnomusicology, pedagogy, music theory, jazz, multiple winds, or collaborative arts).
Combines Integrated Studies with Peabody teacher-licensure coursework for instrumental or vocal/general music. Five-year Blair-to-Peabody track adds the M.Ed. and teacher licensure.

Rigorous music study (≥63 hours) combined with a substantial second focus outside of music (≥18 hours) — self-designed or in fields like pre-medicine, music and the mind, art history, or computer science. Optional 3+2 Blair-to-Owen track adds an MBA from Owen Graduate School of Management.
